textPlain: Before anything one should probably check or at least ballpark their IQ score. The median IQ for mathematics PhD students probably hovers somewhere around 145, about the top 0.2% of the population, correlated with about a 1510/1600 on the SATs, a 34 on the ACTs, etc. Those aren't perfect correlates but you're much more likely to have an SAT or ACT score than a professional IQ score handy.Math is infamously g-loaded, pure math even more so. An unfortunate fact of life. On the bright side, math is very much a "shoot for the moon and you'll land among the stars" subject to pursue if you even loosely keep industrial or business applications in mind.

"Just follow this simple road map".I think about it differently.  If you want to become a pure mathematician, you have to publish research in pure mathematics.  There are many different routes one can take to accomplish this, and the route that you can stick with and enjoy is the best one.
Unfortunately, most links are dead. At least the pages related to real analysis.
Also a great way to lose your mind.
Wow the way this renders on mobile is dizzying

My experience with pure math is that this is not necessary to get job as one, even at good institutions, but you will be terrorized by the arrogance of the ones you mention. Learning to deal with the "brilliant jerk" is a problem in many fields, but the ones I've met in pure math are some of the weirdest (and most vicious)

The interest in pure mathematics probably sets inquiring individuals in a higher intelligence bracket already. If somebody got through high school enjoying and succeeding at geometry and calculus, then they probably could stomach most undergraduate work in the same manner.It seems a bit like gatekeeping to make people question whether they are smart enough when they will figure out pretty quickly if they have the aptitude or will to do it just by being exposed.
Not true at all, we need mathematicians and scientists from all backgrounds, and creativity comes in many shapes

My personal experience: I don't think anyone is naturally smarter than anyone else. Math skill is mostly determined by how much practice/study you put into it.The cases where a person seems more naturally apt than another can be explained by past math experience or by how well they've figured out how to learn/think properly (which is also very much done by practice).Personal Anecdote: In middle school I joined a math circle of sorts with about 20 kids and every weekend we'd do an Olympiad test. When I joined I wasn't even top 10 in the group, but by the next year I was the best one by far and won the major national championships. I didn't study by myself at all, just did the same tests all the other kids did too.I reflect a lot about what happened in that time and me being smarter than the others is never the conclusion I arrive at. I attribute it to a mixture of effort during the test test,having very high standards for what actually understanding a solution meant, liking competition and finding fun in math, which compounded over the dozens of tests we did.You can say those attributes are defined biologically by IQ or similar, but I've personally found them to be highly transferrable/teachable to the people I came to study with.

I could not agree more. I am on my second try to master mathematics (30 years after the first), and I can see, understand and appreciate mathematics mainly from the constructive standpoint.Nothing wrong with classical mathematics, as also used in this roadmap. Having axioms and drawing logical conclusions or searching proof does just not click for me.Give me 0: N and suc: N -> N and I see how to construct stuff. Induction makes sense right away as a case distinction on those two constructors.
